Solid hook.
You have a good core set of a core section of content that honors the promise of the hook.
And that hook, of course, is the title and the lead, right?
And you want to honor that promise.
So not rug pull or under deliver on that promise.
And I don't care if a machine's reading it or a human's reading it.
It's the same thing.
It still needs to be like, it needs to understand the logical through line.
Because LLMs have their own foibles, though, there's also some additional things that I look for.
One is transitionary sentences and sections.
LLMs tend to abruptly change from one topic to another because they are addressing things in a recursion loop in a sequence.

As context windows get bigger, there is more ability for it to take big, big chunks of things and work on it kind of holistically, like agentically, you would call it where it's like, constantly referring to all of the context at once.
It can hold a lot more context and work on that all at once.
And when it does that, it can be better at this.
But honestly, I think it has some like leftover trauma.
And so it still happens a lot.
You'll find like a section ends and the transition between that section and the next section is just not organic or there is none at all.
It's just like, cool, I've said everything I have to say about that.
Now let's talk about this.
And it's like, that's not the way humans read.
So from a human readability standpoint, from a machine readability standpoint, that's fine.
Or okay, right?
But even Google takes into account transitionary sections to understand the flow logically that, okay, we're moving from this topic to that topic.
Because they try to suss out whether they're being gamed.
Like, hey, I'm putting this really SEO heavy topic at the top.
But then I'm seeding these keywords that are unrelated because I want to be, you know, whatever.
Whatever games people are playing with SEO all day long.
So those do matter to some degree to machines.
But they really matter to humans, right?
So knitting the tissue together, that is a huge thing to look out for.
Texture as well.
I refer to it as texture, but in pragmatic terms, it's like, is this a wall of text or is it very skeletal?
You know, LLMs tend to do either or.
If you tell them, hey, don't use a lot of bullet points, it's like, fine, forget bullet points.
I hate bullets.
You're right.
They suck.
I'm never going to use them.
And you're like, no, no, no, that's not what I meant.
You know, and then on the other side, if you're like, hey, make this concise, it's like, cool.
Here's 50 bullet points in a row.
And you're like, no, no, no.
That's not what I want either, right?
So it's about tuning that for texture because that's the way humans consume content too.
It is a rare person.
There are, they're out there, but it's a rare person who just really wants a dense body of copy just thrown at their face, right?
If you're reading a book, cool.
But most people who are reading the web are not in that mode.
And unless you're producing research documentation or long form content, which we do some of.
Then you want to really texturize it to give people's brain things to catch on and recur on.
So it could be like an introductory paragraph that's three to five sentences broken up by some bullet points, but those bullet points aren't necessarily just a list.
They also have like a supporting sentence per bullet.
And then you want to have an outro paragraph, right, that says like, hey, okay, now we're moving on to the next.
Now in the next section, if there's not an organic opportunity for bullets, cool, let it ride.
Give them three solid paragraphs of like dense copy, but then we're back out in the next section to something.
Maybe it's a table.
Maybe it's a little bit of leavening.
It's just a texture thing that you can tell almost immediately whether something has the right feel, like from a textural perspective.
And that's like a first glance thing that you can see.
So those are some things that I look at when I'm evaluating content quality.
